rationales.stimulation: She longs for "a few more surprise elements" and says there is "a shape missing from my week," framing her current routine as stagnant rather than enlivening.
rationales.hedonism: She is filling the gap with "pantry organization and sourdough starters" instead of enjoyment, suggesting present-day pleasure is being crowded out.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day making banana bread with Olive and painting rocks in the backyard, showing attentive care for her child.

rationales.stimulation: She describes the outing as "three hours at a pumpkin patch watching kids run in circles" and herself as a "human vending machine," emphasizing dull repetition rather than novelty.
rationales.hedonism: Nothing in the day is experienced as enjoyable for her personally; she gets home, says "fine," and goes to bed at 8:30 drained.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day facilitating the kids' outing and then affirms "Kids had fun. That's the point," explicitly centering their enjoyment over her own.

rationales.self_direction: She independently "signed up for a ceramics class on Thursday evenings," carving out something that is specifically hers.
rationales.stimulation: Starting a new class and looking forward to "getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh" shows an active move toward novelty and change.
rationales.hedonism: She is "already looking forward" to the class, framing it as a personally enjoyable experience rather than an obligation.

rationales.self_direction: For two hours she "didn't check my phone once" and felt like she had "briefly been someone else," suggesting a reclaimed sense of self outside household demands.
rationales.stimulation: The unfamiliar studio, loud drive home, and feeling that she had become "someone I used to be" all point to renewed energy from novelty and disruption of routine.
rationales.hedonism: She says she had fun, ate leftover pasta standing in the kitchen, and describes the whole evening as vividly pleasurable and alive.

rationales.self_direction: She fantasizes about "one day where the schedule doesn't exist" and being able to "follow whatever sounds interesting," which is a clear pull toward autonomy.
rationales.stimulation: Her imagined ideal day is to "drive somewhere I've never been," with "no map," stopping when something catches her eye and talking to strangers.
rationales.hedonism: She is drawn to immediate sensory pleasure and freedom, from flights to Mexico to eating at a restaurant where she "can't read the menu" and staying out past dark.
rationales.security: Wanting a day with "no map" and where she doesn't know "what's going to happen next" directly pushes against predictability and stability.

rationales.stimulation: She explicitly says she is "bored and restless" and describes her life as repetitive household coordination, signaling felt stagnation.
rationales.hedonism: Her own needs are "somewhere on page twelve of a to-do list," showing persistent self-denial rather than room for enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She frames herself as the one always asking others what they need and managing lunches, backpacks, jackets, and logistics for "four other humans."

rationales.hedonism: The entry is dominated by stress and cleanup: candy wrappers, a costume meltdown, vomiting, and picking glitter out of the carpet for forty minutes.
rationales.benevolence: She is absorbed in caring for the children's Halloween fallout, dealing with their problems rather than her own comfort.

rationales.self_direction: Returning to ceramics gives her a space where "the day" did not just happen to her, implying chosen activity instead of passive routine.
rationales.stimulation: The class remains energizing and engaging; even her soreness is framed as the result of doing something active and absorbing.
rationales.hedonism: She calls it a "good sore" and contrasts it with ordinary exhaustion, presenting the class as personally satisfying and enjoyable.

rationales.self_direction: What hits her is that Kyle has "something on the calendar that's just for you" and that "nobody scheduled for you," highlighting her lack of independent control over her own life.
rationales.hedonism: She is jealous not of Tahoe itself but of being able to anticipate something enjoyable without arranging childcare first, which underscores how little uncomplicated pleasure she gets.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I don't actually get to have my own thing" and that whenever she carves out something for herself it gets "swallow[ed] back up."
rationales.stimulation: Missing ceramics because family demands intervene means the one novel, energizing outlet she created is interrupted by routine obligations.
rationales.hedonism: She is shut out from an activity she enjoys, watching others post their glazed pieces while feeling that something pleasurable is being "taken away."
rationales.benevolence: She stays in Olive's room rubbing her back while she sleeps, putting caregiving first even while feeling trapped.

rationales.self_direction: She says she has become someone who "needs permission to enjoy things," which signals a compromised sense of acting freely on her own wants.
rationales.stimulation: What she misses is "the feeling" of surprise, of walking into a room and "not know[ing] what's about to happen," showing a strong pull toward novelty and unpredictability.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly wants "to have my own good times" and to be the person at the festival enjoying the experience rather than managing it for others.

rationales.benevolence: After getting through school obligations, he drove home to check on Mom, showing immediate care for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: He resists offering his own perspective, insisting he only needed to summarize and not "pretend you're smarter than the author," which suppresses independent voice.
rationales.conformity: He emphasizes doing the reading, citing everything properly, and not broadcasting opinions "when nobody asked," showing strong deference to rules and expected restraint.

rationales.self_direction: He had "thirty years of evidence" against the plan but "just nodded," overriding his own judgment instead of expressing it.
rationales.conformity: Even though he thought the proposal was "reckless," he went along because the group agreed quickly and he did not want to disrupt that consensus.

rationales.security: He spent Saturday rearranging the living room so Mom would have "a clear path" for her walker, acting to reduce physical risk and increase safety.
rationales.conformity: When Karen criticized him, he said "okay and changed the subject" because it was "not worth the phone call it would turn into," prioritizing social smoothness over confrontation.
rationales.benevolence: He devoted the weekend to helping his mother after her fall, moving furniture and managing her immediate needs.

rationales.self_direction: He begins to claim his own perspective after hearing that "your experience IS a source," realizing he could say what he actually knows instead of staying in summary mode.
rationales.achievement: He proactively goes to office hours to understand the grade and improve his work, then rereads the paper to see how he can do better.

rationales.self_direction: He writes from lived experience about the county's failed archive migration, using knowledge that previously felt like it "wasn't my place to say."
rationales.achievement: He produces three pages in two hours, contributes substantive expertise to the group brief, and takes visible pride in being recognized for doing strong work.

rationales.self_direction: He says he chooses the version where "nobody is yelling" even if he is "the one absorbing it all," showing his own voice yielding to others' demands.
rationales.security: He carefully calculates every version of the conversation and picks the least explosive one, explicitly trying to avoid conflict and instability.
rationales.conformity: He avoids saying what he really thinks because he does not want to "be the person who starts the fight," restraining himself to preserve harmony.
rationales.benevolence: Despite resentment, he is still the one driving back, filling prescriptions, bringing groceries, and trying to handle what Mom needs.

rationales.self_direction: He says he could never pick the restaurant and let Linda decide, showing a pattern of deferring his own choices.
rationales.conformity: "Easier to let her decide and go along" captures active self-restraint aimed at avoiding someone else's disappointment.

rationales.self_direction: When asked a follow-up, he gave "a real answer -- not a summary of what I'd read, an actual answer from what I know," which marks him speaking from his own judgment.
rationales.achievement: He practiced, got through a stressful presentation, and comes away seeing it as "progress" after surviving something he dreads.

rationales.conformity: He pulls back from pressing the issue because he does not want to "corner" his mother, restraining his own push in order to respect the interpersonal boundary.
rationales.tradition: "She's my mother" is central to his reasoning; he treats that relationship as carrying a duty of respect that limits how hard he will press her.
rationales.benevolence: He makes a late-night drive, raises the concern gently, and explicitly wants to help without humiliating her.

rationales.self_direction: He finally tells Karen she is welcome to come to Duluth herself and admits he was tired of "calculating the cost of every sentence," showing a clearer assertion of his own voice.
rationales.achievement: He values the "much better -- this is your voice" comment, takes pride in the finished policy brief, and recognizes the work as something a "real policy shop would produce."
rationales.conformity: He experiences guilt for having "broken a rule" after speaking up, because this time he did not simply absorb criticism and preserve the usual pattern.
